replies.author: @UToobUsername01
replies.text:
@L0rdEsedess The size is smaller but the engineering has gotten better especially for the Studio Series line. Studio Series 86 Optimus Prime is the best retail optimus prime ever made and often called a mini-masterpiece toy. The reason for everything getting more expensive is due to the dollar losing its value. Toys stayed the same price but went smaller. So you still got the same quality in engineering but they just made them smaller and have less plastic material. It was a good trade off. 

I would much rather a smaller toy that doesn't suck vs a big one that has low quality engineering. I don't respect Clownfish when it comes to reviewing toys. He is a generic collector that probably doesn't open his toys. (like comic collectors that are not actual fans of the comics but collect them like stamps or pokemon cards ie as an investment only ) 
Clownfish = good for news but shit for actual toy knowledge. He doesn't review transformers, and claims to have hundreds of toys from it. When I told him to review the recent Studio Series 86 toys he didn't respond. That shows he is a generic collector that doesn't even open the box. 😛
This is not a hasbro problem ALL TOYS HAVE GONE UP IN PRICE. I collect Macross Chogokin (the most expensive, detailed and anime-accurate toys from Japan made by Bandai-Namco - these are on the same level of quality of Masterpiece Transformers) and even these have gone up in price despite being already the most expensive stuff on the market!  So it's not hasbro ripping you off, it's your USD dollar losing value thanks to the central bank printing more money like crazy which debases its value. When the dollar loses its value, the business respond by putting prices up because they don't want to give away free stuff. (which makes sense because that is what would happen if they kept the price the same while the central banks print money out of nothing)
Please learn about monetary inflation. It's what happens when your government tells the central banks to print more money into the economy to give the illusion that the economy is ok. In the old days money used to be backed by Gold so they couldn't get away with debasing the dollar as easily. You don't do that anymore and when you go into a bank you won't see a vault with gold bars in it like in the old days. (old cartoons would have super villains break into a bank's vault and grab the gold and jewlery out of the vault and rob the bank. Notice how villain don't do that anymore? Because there is nothing there)

On the topic of detail:
these days putting detail is very easy now since the lack of detail in the older vintage toys was more of a technical issue: your manufactuer tech was limited back then. These days you can make very detailed toys without increasing the price much at all since our technology is leaps and bound better than the stuff in the 1980s. One example is the Studio Series toys from the bayformer movies. These maintain all the detail of the movies but they just made them smaller. (save money on materials due to the dollar losing value) 

When you buy studio series 86 toys they have to make them look toon and show accurate and stick to the simple designs of the original toys and shows. It's not a limitation of the tech, it's just simple because the lineart was simple back then.  Hope that helps explains things. There are many toys that add upon the detail of the original characters (the optimus prime figure from Transformers Bumblebee movie is one awesome example where Optimus Prime looks like G1 self but with a more modernised look) but Studio Series line is trying to be as toon and movie-accurate as possible. If the original design was basic (like the original transformers animated movie) then the toy must maintain that same basic look too in order to be accurate to the movie or show they first appears in. Adding detail is not expensive today dude. Our computer CAD programs are many times more advanced than the 1980s and we have much more memory. :P

If you like Detail-infested toys check out the Transformers Siege line. It's the closest thing to "warhammer meets transformers" aesthetic you can get. This was when toys were starting to get reduced in size but looked a lot more detailed.

​​@Dr.Mlieko good, Western animation is... I have no good words. Institutions full of people who, in chorus, tell me my option doesn't matter don't deserve a penny from me, they haven't earned it. If lots of other people make similar decisions, then they wither into irrelevance. replies.author: @Dr.Mlieko
replies.text: @ElvesLOL the problem is that you only seem to focus on the negatives

replies.author: @ElvesLOL
replies.text:
​@Dr.MliekoI see no downsides when an industry that ruins IP after IP and shows no signs of stopping goes under. Capitalism is after all creative destruction, institutions that fail deserve to go under. The great animation studios of the past have long been forfeit.

For example, whatever culture now occupies Disney, it's not the culture of Walt, the "Nine Old Men", or even Eisner. Just as game studios, such as Bioware and Blizzard get hollowed out as time and turnover take hold, so too with animation studios. It's the people, not the name on the door that makes companies great and these institutions have not choosen good custodians to shepherd them into the future. For whatever reason, Asia seems to do a better job of this but time will tell if that outcome is simply a local aberration.

Regardless, when I can simply watch anime or indie animation, it doesn't affect me at all. Even if animation stopped being produced now and forever, there's more in existence now than one could reasonably watch in a lifetime.

Also, recommend the indie studio have a lawyer ON HAND to make sure that they don't get shafted like "megas XLR" did.

replies.author: @ElvesLOL
replies.text: ​@Dr.MliekoI'm pretty sure having to earn your keep, rather than "to each according to their need", is not a hallmark of commy logic. I'm honestly unclear why you're in favor of preserving malfeasant, rotting institutions rather than letting them pass. When the dead wood burns it makes room for new trees.

replies.text:
@ElvesLOL because then we could apply the same to people

replies.author: @ElvesLOL
replies.text:
​@Dr.Mlieko  If you want to stretch the logic of "failing institutions should be allowed to be abolished by market forces" to something sinister, that is your prerogative. Institutions are not people and I do not subscribe to protectionism or cronyism.

A failing institution is failing, more often than not, because it has a bad culture and bad values. It should be allowed to fail. Unproductive jobs will be eliminated, freeing individuals to find gainful, productive activities. The world is no worse off for want of a milkman because we have refrigeration. The milkmen simply found something else to do.

I really want AI to become a workflow tool that allows animators to take out the busy work and focus on the vision, the quality and the style and just produce more animations consistently or be able to be more ambitious with their animations having the AI assist with the heavy lifting.

author: @Ann-vz8tz
text: In 2020 during the pandemic there was a lot of talk how animation is the future, because it can be done from home and adapted better than live-action to situations like that. And now, only a 5 years, later situation for animation is bad, no ad revenue, tv or youtube, AI dangers, no merch. Things change so fast, we dont know really what would be a situation 5 years from now. Maybe indie boom giving work in new studios, maybe renewed interest in toyetic cartoons or investion in new IPs after people being tired of reboots, maybe changes in youtube. Maybe it will be not good with just current trends continuing. Things can change very fast.

replies.author: @SuperCosmicMutantSquid

author: @Dtoons
text:
Even indie animation channels like Glitch succeed due to being heavily reliant on merch sales.
Whether it’s ratings and ad space on TV, or views and pre-roll ads online. Cartoons had to also rely on licensed goods. Also international sales (dubbing), home video, and streaming deals.

replies.author: @SuperCosmicMutantSquid
replies.text:
That's where they messed up.

They think art can only be serious.

I take Rocko more seriously as art than Steven Universe even though one was trying super hard to act like it was talking shit while the other show is some of the wildest absurdist 90s comedy stuff that actually made a LOT of the same talking points as US but knew how to be tongue and cheek with them so the point actually DOES land vs SU constant talk-a-thons that end up being vapid as hell.

author: @tonyvieson9194
text: I know as a past foster parent, other family, and friends families as well as even our church community. We do not allow our kids to watch the vast majority of animated anything without first reviewing it and if we can't review it first, it's a hard no. Furthermore, it depends on what they are selling. Most things market toward kids lacks imagination and over all interest. Outside side of Legos, there are very few things you even need to talk kids out of or say I don't really want to buy it. Most my kids walked right past the toy isles because they weren't interested any anything being sold. When I as a kid, my parents had to pry us from the toy isle or avoid it all together. If it's not a game or something you play on a tablet, there just isn't any interest.

author: @Earthdadddy
text:
Entertaining video 😂

I’m surprised people fought you on the stuff you mentioned in this video. I don’t watch cartoons except when I feel like reliving my childhood shows, but I’m surprised all these artists weren’t aware of the real state of the industry. I assumed it was public knowledge by industry insiders over the years about the state of things.

Artists should learn how much money is made in media. You cannot go into that industry thinking you just get to draw a cartoon and call it a day. It has to pay for itself long term.

author: @donworland
text: I had no idea they thought of those days ..... just an advertisement.....wow....Robotech was one of the most brilliant projects in that era, and the toy companies had to catch up to that series.  Transformers had constant redesigns, all of that needed artists, people who understood sculpting and industrial production, then came comic books for DECADES.   I did not realize how short sighted present day animators are.  People still have Autobot and Decepticon logo merch.

author: @TheREALSeanKrummel
text: I have no understanding of why you wouldn't want toys and stuff based on your show.

author: @definitelynotapervert5602
text:
You want to know why MLP G4 ran for as long as it did and is still considered the de facto representative of the franchise? Because the character designs were extremely toyetic. The designs were fantastic in how much aesthetic appeal they had and how they brimmed with personality, and that was reflected in the show itself.

When artistic intent and financial longevity work together, it creates something beautiful and truly long lasting beyond it's initial demographic and fanbase. They don't have to be in conflict, it's just a matter of fulfilling both sides of the sector.

It's not like great cartoons still can't be screwed over by lack of toy sales, but to act like a show selling toys is some kind of taboo is simply disingenuous and not feasible.
